description:
Reproducible: with our daemon mounted via -o backend=fskit on macOS 26.4.1 + macFUSE 5.2.0, running 'buck2 build root//src/rust/composition:composition' from inside the mount kills the daemon within ~2.2 seconds. The crash is __stack_chk_fail inside libfuse3.4.dylib at offset 0xcb60 (a function ending around there with 'bl ___stack_chk_fail' at 0xcb5c), called from 0x195b0, called from fuse_session_process_buf_internal (0x177d0). 15 worker threads running concurrently when it triggers.

Daemon-side metrics just before the crash: ~2900 GETATTR, ~1900 LOOKUP, ~1000 STATFS, ~630 MKDIR, ~540 SETATTR, ~410 RELEASE, ~290 OPEN, ~280 FORGET, ~250 SYMLINK, ~150 READDIR, ~150 CREATE, ~110 WRITE — so it processes thousands of ops correctly before something overruns a stack buffer in libfuse's worker thread.

Likely candidates for the overflow:

  * One of our callbacks writes more bytes into a libfuse-allocated stack
    buffer than the buffer is sized. Suspects: fuse_statfs (we write
    sizeof(libc::statvfs)=64 into what macFUSE expects to be a 2168-byte
    struct statfs — under-write, not over-write, so probably not), or
    some Darwin-extended struct we haven't mirrored yet.
  * libfuse 3.18 ABI mismatch we haven't hit yet — e.g. fuse_darwin_attr
    might be smaller in macFUSE's compiled lib than what we declare.
  * A real concurrency bug in macFUSE/libfuse that only surfaces with
    fuse_loop_mt + many parallel ops.

Investigation steps to try next:

  * Build a minimal C program that reproduces the workload (mkdir+symlink
    spam) directly via libfuse and see if it crashes too. Isolates whether
    bug is in our Rust callbacks or in libfuse.
  * Patch fuse_statfs to write libc::statfs (the bigger struct macFUSE
    expects on Darwin) and see if the crash moves.
  * Reduce concurrency by limiting threads via fuse_loop_cfg_set_max_threads.

Once fixed, this unblocks the proper turnkey-4vl.4 benchmark (cold buck2
build through FSKit vs native baseline).
title: macFUSE FSKit libfuse stack overflow under concurrent load (heavy buck2)
